title: PHASE: Pauli Hierarchical Assembly on Subdivided Elements for Quantum-Compatible Operator Synthesis
abstract: Efficiently decomposing finite element stiffness matrices into the Pauli basis is challenging due to the exponential growth of Pauli strings with problem size. A naive Pauli expansion requires $Θ(8^{\lceil \log_2 N \rceil})$ operations, where $N$ denotes the number of degrees of freedom, rendering direct decomposition infeasible for large systems. Existing approaches exploit algebraic sparsity or operator structure but do not incorporate the geometric organization intrinsic to finite element discretizations, and consequently exhibit poor scaling for stiffness matrices. To address this problem, we introduce PHASE, a hierarchical, geometry-aware Pauli decomposition algorithm that leverages recursive mesh partitioning to organize element contributions across multiple spatial scales. PHASE employs a hybrid strategy that combines full- and reduced-space Tensorized Pauli Decomposition with Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform-based aggregation to assemble global Pauli coefficients efficiently. We show that this approach yields a dimension-dependent reduction in the exponential scaling exponent of Pauli assembly asymptotic complexity relative to existing methods, reducing the cost from $2^{2{\lceil \log_2 N \rceil}}$ to $2^{γ_d{\lceil \log_2 N \rceil}}$ with $γ_d < 2$ under standard mesh regularity and balanced partition assumptions. These results substantially improve the feasibility of quantum-compatible operator synthesis for large-scale finite element models.
summary: The paper introduces PHASE, an algorithm designed to efficiently decompose large finite element stiffness matrices into the Pauli basis required for quantum simulation. By leveraging the geometric structure of the mesh through recursive partitioning and combining advanced algebraic techniques like FWHT, it drastically reduces the exponential computational cost associated with naive Pauli expansions.
main_problem: The core challenge is efficiently decomposing large, geometrically structured finite element stiffness matrices into the Pauli basis for quantum-compatible operator synthesis.
main_result: The proposed PHASE algorithm achieves a dimension-dependent reduction in the exponential scaling exponent of the assembly complexity from $2^{2\lceil\log_2 Nceil}$ to $2^{\gamma_d \lceil\log_2 Nceil}$ with $\gamma_d < 2$.
method: PHASE uses a hierarchical, geometry-aware approach combining recursive mesh partitioning (domain decomposition) with a hybrid algebraic strategy involving Tensorized Pauli Decomposition and Fast Walsh-Hadamard Transform aggregation.
model_or_system: The model is the global stiffness matrix ($K$) derived from solving partial differential equations using the Finite Element Method (FEM). This classical operator is mapped onto an $n$-qubit Hilbert space via its unique Pauli basis expansion.
key_observables: Pauli coefficients ($\alpha_P$), asymptotic complexity scaling exponents ($\gamma_d, \hat{\gamma}$), and cut element counts ($|T_{	imes}^{[k]}|$).
important_parameters: Number of degrees of freedom ($N$), number of qubits ($n = \lceil\log_2 Nceil$), mesh regularity, and partition imbalance ratio ($\delta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes standard assumptions for conforming, shape-regular, quasi-uniform meshes; the complexity bounds are derived under specific conditions regarding partition quality.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the recursive separator hierarchy and associated cut sets in the mesh, as well as comparisons of empirical vs. theoretical scaling exponents ($\hat{\gamma}$ vs. $\gamma_{\star}, \gamma_{avg}$).
paper_structure: The paper systematically develops the geometric decomposition (recursive partitioning) to map FEM structure onto a binary address system, then details the hybrid algebraic assembly process using TPD and FWHT aggregation across different depths of the hierarchy.
why_interesting: This work provides a crucial algorithmic breakthrough for simulating quantum systems derived from classical PDEs, offering a pathway to scale quantum simulations beyond current resource limitations by respecting underlying physical geometry.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
